text: This video will demonstrate the laparoscopic approach for a closed-loop small bowel obstruction. A seventy-nine-year-old female presented to the hospital with two days of abdominal pain associated with nausea and vomiting. She had tenderness to palpation in the lower abdomen. Her surgical history included a hysterectomy that had been performed many years in the past. A CT scan was performed which revealed a distended loop of intestine concerning for a closed-loop obstruction.

During laparoscopic exploration, ischemic bowel with necrotic and hemorrhagic mesentery is visible in the pelvis. The surgeon uses a ligature device to transect the adhesion from a prior hysterectomy, unraveling it to relieve the closed-loop obstruction. The bowel is delivered from the pelvis, revealing the extent of injury and one of the transition points. Significant bowel ischemia and mesenteric necrosis are observed, prompting a planned resection.

A twelve millimeter port is placed to accommodate the laparoscopic stapler. The surgeon begins mesenteric transection, creating a window at a level of healthy intestine. The laparoscopic stapler is positioned across the intestine, allowing for precise adjustments. The bowel is transected, and mesenteric dissection continues below the necrotic area to prevent bleeding. The stapler is reintroduced to transect the bowel at another healthy section. Final mesenteric attachments are ligated with an energy device, and the specimen is removed from the operative field.

An assistant helps create a window on the anti-mesenteric border of the small intestine, allowing for the removal of a bowel segment under direct visualization. A similar window is made at the other staple line, and the bowel is aligned in an anti-peristaltic fashion for intracorporeal anastomosis. Two graspers hold the intestine as the stapler is carefully placed to prevent slippage. The common channel is formed and lengthened to approximately ten centimeters, with the anastomosis visualized showing no bleeding.

The surgeon grasps the bowel and uses a stapler to close the common enterotomy, ensuring both sides of the bowel are properly closed. Approximately seventy-five percent of the enterotomy is closed with the first firing of the stapler. The stapler is reintroduced to complete the anastomosis, maintaining the integrity of the initially long common channel.

The completed small bowel anastomosis is visible, showing a widely patent connection. The surgeon grasps the small portion of bowel from the anastomosis closure and the main specimen. The twelve millimeter port site is enlarged slightly, and a wound protector is placed. Using a Babcock clamp through the wound protector, the specimens are extracted without creating a separate extraction site. The pelvis is irrigated and suctioned, and the bowel is examined, confirming the anastomosis is in excellent position with no mesenteric twisting. The two leaves of the small mesenteric defect are grasped and closed using a twelve millimeter clip applier.

The surgeon places clips to close the mesenteric defect completely. The wound protector is removed from the twelve millimeter port site. The large fascial defect is closed using an endoclosed device with absorbable suture in a figure of eight pattern, addressing potential future herniation risks.

The image at the top of the screen shows the patient's postoperative abdomen. The left upper quadrant incision seen in the red circle is the extraction site. Shown at the bottom of the screen is the specimen. This patient had an uneventful postoperative course. On postoperative day one, she was passing flatus and tolerating liquids. Her diet was advanced and she was discharged to her home on post-operative day three. The patient returned to the office two weeks later with no complaints.
This video demonstrated a minimally invasive technique in the emergent setting. With proper experience, this is safe and leads to improved patient outcomes.
